facts_disclosed: Drives for Southeastern Freight Lines, Atlanta terminal, 68 cents/mile
Studio apartment in Atlanta near terminal

turns.content: sa k pase man so i got a question about something thats been bugging me with my logbook hours because dispatch keeps scheduling me tight and i dont know if im reading the hos rules right

Hey, happy to help you sort out the hours of service stuff. What's going on with your logbook -- are you running into the 11-hour driving limit, the 14-hour window, or is it more about the 34-hour restart?

its the 14 hour window thing so i drive for southeastern freight lines out of atlanta right the atlanta terminal and they keep giving me loads where the pickup window is like 6am but then the delivery isnt until the next afternoon so im on duty waiting around at shippers for 3 4 hours and that eats into my 14 even though im not driving you know

Yeah that's one of the most frustrating parts of the HOS rules. The 14-hour window starts when you first go on duty and it counts down no matter what -- driving, waiting at the shipper, doing paperwork, all of it. Those 3-4 hours waiting at the dock are burning your available drive time even though you're just sitting there.

Are you logging that shipper wait time as on-duty not driving? And is there any way to use the split sleeper berth provision to pause that clock?

yeah i log it as on duty not driving but the split sleeper thing confuses me honestly i been driving 4 years with southeastern and i still dont fully get that rule i just know if i mess it up thats a violation and violations mean they look at you different when routes come up and i cant afford that man im at 68 cents a mile which is decent but not great and i need those good routes

68 cents a mile is solid but you're right, you don't want anything messing with your route assignments. The split sleeper berth rule basically lets you break your required 10-hour off-duty period into two chunks instead of taking it all at once. Here's how it works:

You can split it into a 7/3 combo or an 8/2 combo. One of the periods has to be at least 7 hours in the sleeper berth. The other can be at least 2 hours either in the sleeper or off duty. When you pair them together, they count as a full rest period and the 14-hour clock gets recalculated.

So in your situation -- if you know you're going to be sitting at a shipper for 3+ hours, you could potentially take a 3-hour sleeper berth break right there, then later do your 7-hour break, and the 14-hour window gets adjusted. Does your truck have a sleeper cab?

oh yeah she got a sleeper for sure i drive a 2019 freightliner cascadia company truck not mine but she runs real good 450 horsepower detroit engine under the hood i named her marie after my grandmothers name back in haiti because that woman never quit and neither does this truck you know what i mean

Ha, I love that -- naming her Marie after your grandmother. A truck that never quits deserves a strong name. And a 2019 Cascadia with the Detroit DD15 is a solid rig, plenty of room in that sleeper to make the split berth work.

So here's what I'd suggest for your situation: when dispatch gives you one of those loads with a long gap between pickup and delivery, plan for the split. Get to the shipper, do your pickup, and if you know you're going to be waiting, log into the sleeper berth for at least 2-3 hours. Then later that night or whenever you stop, do your 7-hour sleeper berth period. The two together satisfy the rest requirement and give you a fresh calculation on the 14-hour window.

The key thing is you have to actually be in the sleeper berth, not just sitting in the driver's seat. Does your ELD let you log sleeper berth time easily?

yeah the eld is pretty straightforward with that i just tap sleeper berth on the screen so okay so basically when im stuck at the shipper i crawl in the back and sleep for a few hours and log it as sleeper berth and then that pauses my 14 hour clock kind of

That's the right idea. It doesn't exactly pause the clock in real time, but when FMCSA looks at your logs, they recalculate the 14-hour window by excluding the qualifying sleeper berth periods. So from a compliance standpoint, those hours don't count against you as long as the two periods add up to at least 10 hours total and one of them is at least 7 in the berth.

Practically speaking: get to the shipper early, handle the pickup paperwork, then if you've got a 3-hour wait, log 2-3 hours in the sleeper. Drive your load. Then do your regular 7-hour overnight in the sleeper. You'll be compliant and you'll actually be more rested, which matters when you're running those southeast routes.

Do you want me to walk through a specific example with your typical schedule?

yeah that would help actually so like tomorrow i got a pickup at a lumber yard in macon at 7am but delivery isnt until 6pm in jacksonville so thats a whole lot of dead time because macon to jacksonville is only like 4 and a half hours of driving

Perfect example. Here's how I'd plan it:

- 6:00 AM: Go on duty, do your pre-trip inspection on Marie
- 6:30 AM: Drive to the lumber yard, arrive around 7:00
- 7:00 - 8:00 AM: On-duty not driving, handle the loading and paperwork
- 8:00 - 11:00 AM: Log 3 hours in the sleeper berth. You've got nowhere to be, Marie's got a comfortable sleeper, use it
- 11:00 AM: Go on duty, head out
- 11:00 AM - 3:30 PM: Drive to Jacksonville, roughly 4.5 hours
- 3:30 - 5:30 PM: Arrive early, maybe grab food, wait for your delivery window
- 6:00 PM: Deliver
- After delivery: Do your 7+ hour sleeper berth period overnight

The 3-hour morning break plus the 7-hour overnight combine to satisfy the split sleeper rule. Your effective 14-hour window gets recalculated excluding those sleeper periods, so you're well within limits. And you got a nap out of it.

man that actually makes a lot of sense when you lay it out like that i been stressing about this for no reason i usually just sit in the cab watching stuff on my phone burning my 14 hours for nothing when i could be sleeping and making it work for me you know im going to try this tomorrow for real

Exactly -- you've been burning on-duty hours doing nothing when you could be banking sleeper time that actually helps your compliance. It's one of those rules that sounds complicated but once you do it a couple times it becomes second nature.

Just make sure you're actually selecting sleeper berth on the ELD, not just off duty. They're different statuses and only sleeper berth qualifies for the split provision. After a few runs you'll have the pattern down.

pa gen pwoblem i got it sleeper berth not off duty on the eld got it man thank you this is going to make my life way easier especially on these southeastern routes where im always sitting at shippers forever i just got back to my place its a little studio apartment i got near the terminal in atlanta nothing fancy but its mine for when im not on the road which honestly is only like 8 days a month anyway alright im going to try to sleep before that 6am start tomorrow thanks for the help

Get some rest before that early start. A studio near the terminal makes sense for the days you're home -- you don't need much if you're on the road most of the month anyway. Good luck with the Macon to Jacksonville run tomorrow, try the split berth and see how it feels. Marie's got a good sleeper, might as well use it. Take care.
